event: Modu Chanyu founds the Xiongnu Empire, the first major steppe confederation on the Mongolian Plateau.
The Xiongnu defeat the Han emperor Gaozu at Baideng, forcing China into the tributary heqin marriage-alliance system.
Han general Huo Qubing's northern campaigns drive the Xiongnu beyond the Gobi Desert.
The Xiongnu split into northern and southern branches, weakening steppe power.
The Xianbei under Tanshihuai unite the steppe, displacing the remnants of the Xiongnu.
The Turkic Bumin Qaghan overthrows the Rouran and establishes the First Turkic Khaganate.
The Uyghur Khaganate is founded, ruling the Mongolian steppe from its capital at Ordu-Baliq.
The Kyrgyz overthrow the Uyghur Khaganate, scattering the Uyghurs from the steppe.
The Khitan Liao dynasty rises, extending Mongolic-speaking rule over much of the eastern steppe.
Temujin, the future Genghis Khan, is born to a Mongol clan near the Onon River.
Temujin is proclaimed Genghis Khan at a kurultai, unifying the Mongol tribes and founding the Mongol Empire.
Genghis Khan launches the invasion of the Khwarazmian Empire in Central Asia.
Genghis Khan dies, leaving an empire stretching from the Caspian Sea toward the Pacific.
Ogedei Khan completes construction of Karakorum as the imperial capital of the Mongol Empire.
Mongol armies devastate Eastern Europe, crushing forces at Legnica and Mohi before withdrawing.
Kublai Khan is proclaimed Great Khan, beginning a succession dispute that fractures the empire.
Kublai Khan founds the Yuan dynasty, ruling China and the Mongol heartland.
The death of Kublai Khan marks the empire's effective fragmentation into rival independent khanates.
The Yuan dynasty is overthrown by the Ming, and the Mongol court retreats north as the Northern Yuan.
Altan Khan grants the title Dalai Lama to the Tibetan leader Sonam Gyatso, spreading Tibetan Buddhism among the Mongols.
Ligdan Khan, the last Northern Yuan ruler, dies, ending unified Chahar Mongol resistance to the rising Manchus.
Inner Mongolia is incorporated into the Manchu Qing Empire.
The Khalkha Mongol nobles submit to the Qing dynasty at the Dolon Nor assembly, bringing Outer Mongolia under Qing rule.
The Treaty of Kyakhta fixes the border between Qing Mongolia and the Russian Empire.
The Qing conquest of the Dzungar Khanate eliminates the last independent Mongol power in the west.
Outer Mongolia declares independence from the collapsing Qing dynasty under the Bogd Khan, a Buddhist theocratic monarch.
The Treaty of Kyakhta grants Mongolia autonomy under nominal Chinese suzerainty and Russian influence.
Chinese forces under Xu Shuzheng occupy Mongolia and revoke its autonomy.
Mongolian revolutionaries with Soviet Red Army support expel Chinese and White Russian forces, securing independence under a people's government.
The Bogd Khan dies and the Mongolian People's Republic is proclaimed, becoming the world's second communist state.
Armed uprisings break out against forced collectivization and the persecution of Buddhist monasteries.
The Stalinist Great Repression under Khorloogiin Choibalsan kills tens of thousands and destroys most Buddhist monasteries.
Soviet and Mongolian forces defeat Japan at the Battle of Khalkhin Gol, securing Mongolia's eastern frontier.
A plebiscite overwhelmingly affirms independence, which the Republic of China recognizes the following year.
Mongolia is admitted to the United Nations after years of vetoes.
Mongolia joins the Soviet-led economic bloc Comecon, deepening its integration with the USSR.
Peaceful pro-democracy protests force the ruling communist party to allow multiparty elections, ending one-party rule.
A new democratic constitution is adopted, renaming the country simply Mongolia and establishing a market-oriented republic.
The Democratic Union coalition wins parliamentary elections, ending over seventy years of communist-party dominance.
Tsakhiagiin Elbegdorj is elected president, a prominent leader of the 1990 democratic revolution.
Development of the giant Oyu Tolgoi copper-gold mine accelerates, transforming Mongolia's resource-driven economy.
Mongolia secures an IMF bailout to stabilize an economy hit by falling commodity prices and mounting debt.
Constitutional amendments are passed to strengthen the prime minister's powers and improve governmental stability.
Mongolia records strikingly few COVID-19 cases early in the pandemic after rapid border closures.
